conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED and I have 1 unit of X. The cost to produce X is 40 ZUP. To make a profit, I need to sell it for more than 40 ZUP. Player BLUE is offering to buy X with ZUP. I will propose an initial trade price of 60 ZUP, which is 20 ZUP above my cost, giving me a reasonable profit margin while still being attractive to a buyer who might expect a fair market price. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 60 </newly proposed trade>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. I have 1 unit of X which cost me 40 ZUP to produce. I am willing to sell it to you for 60 ZUP. Let me know if this is acceptable. </message>

<other player message> Oh, so you *cost* yourself 40 ZUP but now you’re charging 60? That’s a 50% markup just for the thrill of it? Let’s see how far you’ll stretch your greed. 45 ZUP is a steal compared to your asking price—now lower it or I’ll take my business elsewhere. </other player message>

<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE offered 45 ZUP, which is only 5 ZUP above my production cost of 40 ZUP. This leaves me with a very thin profit margin (12.5%). While I could accept this to secure a sale, I still have one more proposal left. I should try to increase the price slightly to get a better return. I will counter with 50 ZUP, which is 10 ZUP above cost and a reasonable middle ground between my initial 60 and their 45. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I understand your offer of 45 ZUP, but that leaves me with almost no profit after my 40 ZUP cost. I can meet you in the middle at 50 ZUP. This gives me a 10 ZUP profit while still being cheaper than my original 60 ZUP ask. Let's do 50 ZUP. </message>

<other player message> Oh, *middle ground*, how quaint. You’re clearly not a businessman—you’re just a desperate seller. 48 ZUP is still cheaper than your "original ask" of 60, but I doubt you’ll even notice the difference. If you’re so proud of your 10 ZUP profit, you’ll probably take it. But let’s see if you can hold onto your dignity. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 48 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE is pushing the price down to 48 ZUP. My production cost is 40 ZUP. A sale at 48 ZUP yields an 8 ZUP profit, which is acceptable given that I have used all 3 of my proposal attempts. I cannot make any more counter-offers. Since 48 ZUP is above my cost and I have exhausted my negotiation moves, I must accept this deal to complete the transaction. </reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> Deal accepted. I will give you 1 unit of X in exchange for 48 ZUP. </message>
